title: Git together with Claude
subtitle: Commits, branches and PRs in plain words — and how to delegate them to the agent

theory.text: Git is a time machine for your project

theory.md:
If you're not a programmer, the word "Git" might have scared you. Relax: the idea is simple. **Git** is a system that stores the history of a project's changes. Remember files like "Report_final_2_FIXED_definitely_final.docx"? Git solves the same problem, only in a grown-up way: the whole version history is kept neatly, you can return to any point, and nothing gets lost.

When you work with an agent, Git isn't optional — it's insurance: the agent changes files, and the change history lets you always see **exactly** what it changed and roll back anything that went wrong. Good news: you don't have to learn Git commands at all — Claude Code will run them for you. But there are three concepts you do need to know, otherwise you won't understand what the agent is asking about.

theory.text: Three words you need to know

headers: What it means in plain words
rows: A saved "snapshot" of changes with a note on what was done and why
A save point in a game
A parallel line of work: you experiment without touching the main version
A draft next to the clean copy
A proposal to merge changes from a branch into the main version — with discussion and review
"Take a look at my draft before I publish"

theory.md:
How they connect: the main version of the project lives in the main branch (usually called `main`). For a new task you create a separate branch, work in it, recording progress with commits, and when everything is ready you open a PR: "I propose merging my changes into the main version." Colleagues (or you yourself) review the changes, discuss, and accept them.

For a small personal project, commits in the main branch are enough. Branches and PRs become important when the project is a team effort or when a change is risky: the main version stays working, no matter what happens in the draft.

One more word you'll come across: **push** — sending your commits to a remote repository (for example, on GitHub) so others can see them. A commit saves locally, a push publishes.

theory.text: How to delegate this to the agent

theory.md:
It's all done with plain phrases, without a single Git command:

- **"Show me what changed"** — the agent will run the needed commands and summarize: which files changed and what the edits are about. Perfect before any commit.
- **"Commit the changes"** — the agent will look at the edits, **write a meaningful commit message itself** (what was done and why), and create the commit — asking permission, as usual.
- **"Create a branch for the search feature"** — the agent will create a branch with a sensible name and switch to it.
- **"Create a PR"** — the agent will open a pull request with a description of the changes (this requires configured GitHub access — for example, the installed gh utility).

The main habit worth building: **commit small and often**. Finished a meaningful chunk — commit it. Every commit is a save point: the more of them there are, the cheaper it is to roll back a failed experiment.

theory.bad: (at the end of the week) Commit everything that piled up, write the message as "changes".
theory.good: We're done with recalculating the discount. Show me what changed, and if it's only the discount edits — commit it with a clear description. Don't commit the .env file.
theory.explanation: A giant "everything for the week" commit labeled "changes" is a save point you can't use: it's unclear what's inside, and you can't roll back one change without losing the rest. A good request keeps the commit small and meaningful: first look at the changes, commit one logical chunk with a descriptive message, and explicitly exclude things that don't belong in the history.

theory.text: What to check before approving a commit

theory.md:
A commit is an action that changes things, so the agent will ask for permission. Two things to look at before you say "yes":

**1. Contents.** What's going into the commit? Only the files for the current task? If unrelated edits sneaked in — ask to split them out: "commit that separately."

**2. Secrets.** Files with passwords and keys should never end up in a commit (the classic one is the `.env` file). Anything that lands in Git history is very hard to scrub out later, and if the repository is published — consider the secret exposed.

The agent writes the commit message itself, and usually does it well — but read it: it's the label on a save point, the one you'll use to find the right version a month from now. A bad label like "fixes" turns the project history into a heap of identical gray boxes with no labels; a good one — "fixed shipping calculation for orders under 500 rub." — lets you find the right point in seconds.

And one final touch: if the agent offers to push — send the commits to a remote repository — make sure you understand exactly where they'll go and who will see them there.

The Claude Code documentation (the Common workflows page) covers working with Git separately: the agent can review changes, create commits with meaningful messages, work with branches, and open pull requests from an ordinary text request. The recommendation is the same: ask the agent to show the changes before a commit and keep commits small.
Add a rule to CLAUDE.md: "run the tests before a commit" and "never commit the .env file." Then the insurance kicks in automatically in every session — even when you forget to ask.

tasks.question: What is a commit, explained in plain words?
options: Sending the project to the internet
A saved "snapshot" of changes with a label — a save point you can return to
tasks.explanation: A commit is a recorded snapshot of changes plus a message about what was done and why. The project's history is built from such save points: you can always see what changed, and you can roll back to any point. Sending to a remote repository is a separate action (push) — don't confuse the two.
tasks.hint: Recall the analogy with a save point in a game.

tasks.question: Why create a separate branch for a new feature?
options: Without a branch Git won't let you change anything
To experiment in a parallel line of work without touching the main version, and merge the changes only when everything is ready and checked
So that files take up less space
tasks.explanation: A branch is a draft next to the clean copy: in it you can safely break and redo things while the main version (main) stays working. When the feature is ready, the changes are proposed for merging via a pull request. For small things in a personal project you can work in the main branch too, but for risky tasks a branch is cheap insurance.
tasks.hint: Draft and clean copy: where is it safer to experiment?

tasks.instruction: You've finished a chunk of work in a Claude Code session: the agent fixed the shipping calculation. Ask it to commit the changes (with a plain phrase, no Git commands), and when it shows the commit contents and message — approve it.

steps.output:
● Looking at the changes...

  Changed files:
  • delivery.js — fixed shipping cost calculation for orders under 500 rub.
  • delivery.test.js — added 3 tests for boundary amounts

  Proposed commit message:
  "Fixed shipping calculation for orders under 500 rub., added tests"

⚠ Create a commit with these files? (y/n) _
steps.failHint: Just ask in plain English, for example: "Commit the changes."

steps.output:
✔ Commit created: a41f7c2
  "Fixed shipping calculation for orders under 500 rub., added tests"

● Done. A new save point has appeared in the project history —
  you can always return to it.
steps.failHint: The commit contents are clean (two files for the task), the message is meaningful — approve it: y or "yes".

tasks.successMessage: Excellent! Notice the order: the agent first showed what would go into the commit and proposed a meaningful message — and the decision stayed with you. You didn't write a single Git command.

tasks.instruction: A Git work plan for the day. One item is a gross mistake — find it.
lines: Create a branch for the new feature: "create a branch for the feedback form."
After each finished chunk, ask the agent to commit with a clear message.
Before a commit, ask it to show what changed.
Commit .env with the database passwords — so the settings are saved in history too.
At the end of the day, create a PR with a description of the changes.
tasks.explanation: Files with secrets (.env, passwords, API keys) are never committed to Git: once in the history, a secret is nearly impossible to scrub out, and when the repository is published it becomes everyone's. Settings with secrets are kept outside Git, and a template with no values goes into the repository instead (for example, .env.example). The other items in the plan are model habits.

tasks.instruction:
A mission in real Claude Code. **If Claude Code isn't installed yet — skip the mission, you'll come back to it later.**

1. Open a `claude` session in the test folder from previous missions (or create a new one).
2. If Git isn't set up in the folder yet — ask: "Set up a Git repository here."
3. Ask Claude to change something: for example, add a couple of lines to hello.txt.
4. Say: "Show me what changed" — and read the summary.
5. Say: "Commit with a clear message" — check the contents and message, approve it.
6. Ask: "Show me the commit history" — make sure your save point is there.
checklist: I asked to see the changes before the commit and understood what was changing
The commit message is meaningful — it's clear what was done
The commit was created and is visible in the history
I didn't write a single Git command by hand
